name: Log cabin joint
description: Horizontal log wall whose courses interlock at the corners through protruding notches, producing the unmistakable silhouette of a hand-hewn log cabin. Each course is a single ring of horizontally-laid logs (oriented along x on the long faces and along z on the short faces, switching axis each course so the grain reads cross-wise at the joint). Corners extend one cell beyond the perimeter, alternating direction each course to weave together — the notch geometry varies by style variant (saddle, square, or dovetail). Optional clay/mud chinking fills the inter-log seam on courses that read as flush. Used for cabins, watchtower lower halves, lumberyards, and viking longhouses where the joinery itself is the architectural statement.
